pattern: Collapsing the second word list into a single per-letter maximum requirement lets each candidate be checked against one combined frequency profile instead of every word individually.
hints: A candidate must cover the heaviest demand for each letter across the second list, not each word separately.
Merge the second list into one requirement by taking, for every letter, the largest count any single word needs.
Build that merged letter-count once, then keep each first-list word whose own letter counts meet or exceed every entry of the merged requirement.

edge_inputs.description: single-letter demands keep words containing both letters
doubled-letter demand narrows to one survivor
merged requirement needs two of one letter
no candidate satisfies the combined demand
every candidate is universal when demand is trivial
single candidate exactly meeting a multiplicity demand
multiplicity matters, a word with one copy fails a two-copy demand

stress.description: random short lowercase words on both sides over a tiny alphabet so survivors vary
stress.note: words1 entries are unique per the constraints; tiny alphabet makes universality non-trivial
